system_prompt: Instruction: You are an annotator who is developing a dataset for the task of identifying main characters in a news article. Your answer should be in JSON using the following format: {'answer': ['character0', 'character1', ... ]}. Do not generate anything else
question: Please list all important characters present in the below article about gun control issues.

demos.article_text: Concealed carriers? Colorado's proposed concealed-carry law would keep names of  permit-holders secret, so if someone is arrested in a shooting,  we'd never know if they had been issued a permit. It sounds as though gun defenders are trying to prevent bad  publicity before it even happens - talk about thinking ahead. The proposal is obviously of concern to the media: It's our  job to tell you the how and the why following a crime or tragic  incident. But this should not be just a media concern. The general  public, too, should have a right to know who's packing. In addition, sheriffs and police chiefs should be held  accountable if mistakes are made and criminals are issued  concealed-carry permits. The measure now pending in the Legislature, HB 1114,  already has passed the full House and a Senate committee, and  would assure that the names of those granted concealed-carry  permits are kept confidential. The bill, backed by Republicans and  being aggressively pushed by the gun lobby, still needs Senate approval. We implore the Senate to kill this bill. If that doesn't  happen, we hope Gov. Bill Owens will veto it as soon as it reaches  his desk. Backers of the bill say only law-abiding residents are able  to obtain permits to carry concealed weapons and they shouldn't  have their names released. But that wasn't the case in Florida.   That state passed a concealed-carry law, and a newspaper there  discovered that nearly 100 permit-holders had arrest records.

Leahy could slow Obama's efforts on gun control President Obama has promised quick action to get a series of gun-control proposals through Congress and has been moving purposefully to that end. But one of his key allies on Capitol Hill, Sen. Patrick J. Leahy (D-Vt.), could prove to be an early obstacle to the president's swift pace. Leahy, who chairs the Senate Judiciary Committee and will be at the White House on Wednesday when Obama unveils proposals from Vice President Biden to limit gun violence, said he wants to hear from more than just the administration. Leahy said he plans to field ideas from a broad range of voices before advancing any legislation through the committee. "I want everybody to be heard," Leahy said in an interview Tuesday, despite potential pressure from the White House to act quickly. "I have some ideas of things that can be done, and if there are things in the vice president's proposal that the committee agrees with, then they'll be included in the legislation. If not, they won't be." Leahy's decision to remain at the helm of the Judiciary Committee, instead of seeking the powerful Appropriations Committee gavel, was greeted with disappointment by some gun-control advocates, who worry his centrist views on gun issues will make him an insufficient advocate for bold action. Advocates blame Leahy for stalling efforts to push new restrictions after the shooting rampage two years ago in which then-Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D-Ariz.) was wounded.

LIST YOUR GUNS ON YOUR 1040? NO -- THIS BILL NEVER PASSED You must list all your guns on your 2010 tax return. Chain e-mail on Sunday, November 1st, 2009 in an e-mail circulated to many people. THE RULING: PANTS ON FIRE * * * It's a scary time to be a gun owner in the United States - at least if you believe some of the claims made by gun-rights groups and spread in chain e-mails. We recently investigated a claim made by Gun Owners of America(http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/statements/2009/oct/20/gun-owner s-america/gun-rights-group-says-health-care-bill-could-harm-/) that a version of a health care bill currently in Congress "could be used to ban guns in home self-defense," and rated it False. We gave the National Rifle Association a Pants on Fire for making a similar claim(http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/statements/2008/sep/23/national-ri fle-association/yes-and-outlaw-apple-pie-as-well/) during the 2008 election. So we were skeptical when a reader forwarded us a chain e-mail that claims the following: "Now 'all Guns' must be listed on your next (2010) tax return! As if we didnt [sic] have enough to get upset about! If you have a gun, I hope it isn't registered! Senate Bill SB-2099 will require us to put on our 2009 1040 federal tax form all guns that you have or own. It will require fingerprints and a tax of $50 per gun." The e-mail continued: "This bill was introduced on Feb.. 24, 2009, by the Obama staff. BUT... this bill will only become public knowledge 30 days after the new law becomes effective! This is an amendment to the Internal Revenue Act of 1986. This means that the Finance Committee has passed this without the Senate voting on it at all. Trust Obama ? ...... you must be kidding!"

VICTORY WITH NO SHOTS FIRED THE Supreme Court gave gun control advocates a victory last week by deciding not to hear a case. It let stand an appeals court ruling that a ban on private possession of handguns in Morton Grove, Ill., is constitutional. Three groups from the Chicago suburb, with support from the National Rifle Association, challenged the 1981 ordinance on the ground that it violated Second Amendment guarantees of the right to bear arms. The High Court has issued only two interpretations of the amendment, ruling in 1886 that it only limits Federal legislation and in 1939 that it does not block prohibition of interstate commerce in sawed-off shotguns. The action not to hear the case creates no legal precedent, and therefore may be of limited significance outside Morton Grove. Even in Illinois, the State Supreme Court has yet to decide a challenge based on the state constitution. Indeed, rifle association attorneys played down the High Court's action, saying the issue was primarily a matter of state law. Last year, a California appeals court overturned a San Francisco law that generally banned the possession of pistols, ruling that the city had no authority to enact it. With numerous other jurisdictions considering similar bans, however, a Supreme Court decision to review the Morton Grove case could have thrown gun control efforts into temporary limbo. A Gallup survey earlier this year indicated that a majority of Americans favor stricter controls on pistol sales, but stop short of calling for an outright ban. In general, the poll found that residents of cities with populations over 50,000 favored bans, while those living in smaller cities and rural areas did not. President Reagan has consistently said that pistol controls are ineffective.

Gun debate rages near Connecticut store where Nancy Lanza bought AR-15 EAST WINDSOR, Conn.    Customers at the Sky Diner in East Windsor on Friday didn't know much about the neighboring gun store that was raided by federal agents the night before. But they did know where they stood on gun control: Like the rest of the nation, very far apart. The television behind the counter flashed with news of funerals and the debate about the reasons why Adam Lanza chose to kill 20 children and six faculty members at Sandy Hook Elementary School with the AR-15 rifle police say his mother, Nancy Lanza, bought at Riverview Gun Sales in the same sprawling shopping plaza. Among those with eyes trained to the TV, was a man named Aaron, who would not give his last name, "for obvious political reasons." Aaron had gone to the gun shop to browse, but, turned away, settled for breakfast and heated discussion. What did he think the country should do about guns in the face of violence? "Absolutely nothing," he said. It is schools that need to change by putting armed guards and metal detectors, he said, echoing suggestions the National Rifle Association made Friday. Denielle Hitchcock, a waitress and mother of young children, argued back. "I don't think people with mental health problems should be able to get guns," she said. Aaron urged her, "You've got to think logically, not emotionally."
